epigraph.text:
He stretches out the north over the void and hangs the 
earth on nothing.

content.text:
In Chapter 1, we met the trigonometric functions—functions defined by
the geometry of the *circle* $x^2 + y^2 = 1$. In this final section
of the chapter, we introduce a family of functions defined by an analogous
relationship with the *hyperbola* $x^2 - y^2 = 1$. These are the
**hyperbolic functions**, and despite their exotic appearance, they are
nothing more than combinations of the exponential functions we studied in
Section 7.1.

The hyperbolic functions arise naturally in engineering, physics, and
architecture. A freely hanging cable—such as a power line or a suspension
bridge chain—takes the shape of a hyperbolic cosine curve, not a
parabola as one might guess. This curve, called a *catenary*, is one
of the most beautiful examples of mathematics describing creation.

content.title: Hyperbolic Sine and Cosine
content.content:
The **hyperbolic sine** and **hyperbolic cosine** are defined by
\[
 \sinh x = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2}, \qquad
 \cosh x = \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2}.
\]

content.text:
The remaining four hyperbolic functions are defined in terms of sinh and
cosh, exactly as the remaining trig functions are defined in terms of sine
and cosine:

content.title: The Other Hyperbolic Functions
content.content:
\[
 \tanh x = \frac{\sinh x}{\cosh x} = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{e^x + e^{-x}},
 \qquad
 \coth x = \frac{\cosh x}{\sinh x}, \qquad
 \operatorname{sech} x = \frac{1}{\cosh x}, \qquad
 \operatorname{csch} x = \frac{1}{\sinh x}.
\]

content.text: Let us examine the basic properties of $\sinh x$ and $\cosh x$:

items:
$\sinh(-x) = -\sinh x$ (odd function) and $\cosh(-x) = \cosh x$
 (even function), mirroring sine (odd) and cosine (even).

As $x \to \infty$, $\sinh x \to \infty$ and $\cosh x \to \infty$;
 both grow like $e^x/2$.
$\cosh x \ge 1$ for all $x$ (its minimum value is $1$ at $x = 0$).

content.caption: Graphs of the hyperbolic functions
content.alt: Graphs of the three main hyperbolic functions plotted on the same coordinate axes. The hyperbolic cosine y = cosh(x) is shown as a U-shaped curve that opens upward, symmetric about the y-axis, with minimum value 1 at x = 0. The hyperbolic sine y = sinh(x) passes through the origin and increases from left to right, resembling a stretched cubic curve (odd function). The hyperbolic tangent y = tanh(x) also passes through the origin but has horizontal asymptotes at y = 1 and y = −1. Key features labeled include the minimum of cosh at (0, 1) and the asymptotic behavior of tanh.

content.text:
The hyperbolic functions satisfy identities strikingly similar to the
trigonometric ones, but with occasional sign changes. The most important
is the hyperbolic Pythagorean identity.

Compare this to $\cos^2 x + \sin^2 x = 1$. The plus sign becomes a minus
sign—the one fundamental difference between circular and hyperbolic
geometry.

Since the hyperbolic functions are built from exponentials, their derivatives
follow directly.

content.title: Hyperbolic Derivatives
content.content:
$$\begin{aligned}
\deriv{}{x}[\sinh x] &= \cosh x
 & \deriv{}{x}[\cosh x] &= \sinh x \\[4pt]
 \deriv{}{x}[\tanh x] &= \operatorname{sech}^2 x
 & \deriv{}{x}[\coth x] &= -\operatorname{csch}^2 x \\[4pt]
 \deriv{}{x}[\operatorname{sech} x] &= -\operatorname{sech} x\,\tanh x
 & \deriv{}{x}[\operatorname{csch} x] &= -\operatorname{csch} x\,\coth x
\end{aligned}$$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We verify the first two. For $\sinh x$:
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\left[\frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2}\right]
 = \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2} = \cosh x.
\]
For $\cosh x$:
\[
 \deriv{}{x}\left[\frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2}\right]
 = \frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2} = \sinh x.
\]
Notice the beautiful pattern: the derivative of $\sinh$ is $\cosh$, and the
derivative of $\cosh$ is $\sinh$—no sign change! Compare to $(\sin x)' =
\cos x$ and $(\cos x)' = -\sin x$ in the trigonometric case.

content.title: Differentiating hyperbolic functions
content.solution:
(a) By the Chain Rule, $f'(x) = \sinh(3x) \cdot 3 = 3\sinh(3x)$.

(b) By the Product Rule,
$g'(x) = \tanh x + x\,\operatorname{sech}^2 x$.

(c) $h'(x) = \dfrac{\sinh x}{\cosh x} = \tanh x$.

content.text:
Since $\sinh x$ is one-to-one on all of $\R$ and $\tanh x$ is one-to-one
on all of $\R$, their inverses exist without domain restriction. For
$\cosh x$, we restrict to $[0, \infty)$ where it is increasing.

content.title: Inverse Hyperbolic Functions
content.content:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sinh^{-1} x &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2+1}\right),
 &\quad& x \in \R. \\
 \cosh^{-1} x &= \ln\!\left(x + \sqrt{x^2-1}\right),
 &\quad& x \ge 1. \\
 \tanh^{-1} x &= \frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right),
 &\quad& -1 < x < 1.
\end{aligned}$$

content.text:
These logarithmic formulas come from solving $y = \sinh x$, $y = \cosh x$,
etc., for $x$ using the quadratic formula on $e^x$.

content.title: Deriving the formula for $\sinh^{-1} x$
content.solution:
Let $y = \sinh^{-1} x$, so $\sinh y = x$:
\[
 \frac{e^y - e^{-y}}{2} = x \implies e^y - e^{-y} = 2x.
\]
Multiplying by $e^y$: $e^{2y} - 1 = 2x\,e^y$, i.e.,
$e^{2y} - 2x\,e^y - 1 = 0$. This is a quadratic in $e^y$:
\[
 e^y = \frac{2x \pm \sqrt{4x^2+4}}{2} = x \pm \sqrt{x^2+1}.
\]
Since $e^y > 0$ and $\sqrt{x^2+1} > \abs{x}$, only the $+$ sign gives a
positive value. Therefore $e^y = x + \sqrt{x^2+1}$, so
$y = \ln(x + \sqrt{x^2+1})$.

content.text: The derivatives of the inverse hyperbolic functions are remarkably clean:

content.content:
For $\sinh^{-1} x$: Let $y = \sinh^{-1} x$, so $\sinh y = x$.
Differentiating: $\cosh y \cdot y' = 1$, hence
$y' = 1/\cosh y = 1/\sqrt{1+\sinh^2 y} = 1/\sqrt{1+x^2}$.

content.text:
These derivatives yield corresponding integral formulas:
\[
 \int\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}\dx = \sinh^{-1} x + C
 = \ln\!\left(x+\sqrt{x^2+1}\right) + C,
\]
\[
 \int\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2-1}}\dx = \cosh^{-1} x + C
 = \ln\!\left(x+\sqrt{x^2-1}\right) + C, \qquad x > 1.
\]

A flexible, uniform chain (or cable) hanging freely under
gravity takes the shape
\[
 y = a\cosh\!\left(\frac{x}{a}\right),
\]
where $a$ is a constant depending on the tension and weight per unit length.
This curve is called a **catenary**, from the Latin *catena*
(chain).

content.title: The Catenary Problem (1690--1691)
content.content:
In 1690, Jacob Bernoulli posed the problem of finding the shape of a hanging
chain. Galileo had conjectured it was a parabola, but this was disproven
by Joachim Jungius in 1669. In 1691, three mathematicians—Leibniz,
Huygens, and Johann Bernoulli—independently found the correct answer:
the catenary $y = a\cosh(x/a)$. The solution required the recently
invented calculus and the hyperbolic cosine function. The Gateway Arch in
St.\ Louis is a weighted catenary (an inverted catenary, to be precise),
as are many suspension bridge cables under their own weight.

content.title: Arc length of a catenary
content.problem: Find the arc length of $y = a\cosh(x/a)$ from $x = 0$ to $x = b$.
content.solution:
From Chapter 6, the arc length is $L = \defint{0}{b}\sqrt{1+(y')^2}\dx$.
We have $y' = \sinh(x/a)$, so
$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \defint{0}{b}\sqrt{1+\sinh^2(x/a)}\dx
 = \defint{0}{b}\sqrt{\cosh^2(x/a)}\dx \\
 &= \defint{0}{b}\cosh(x/a)\dx
 = a\sinh(x/a)\Big|_0^b
 = a\sinh(b/a).
\end{aligned}$$
The identity $\cosh^2 u - \sinh^2 u = 1$ made the square root collapse
perfectly—a fitting conclusion to a chapter about inverse functions and
the beautiful relationships between them.

marginNotes.text:
When he established the heavens, I was
there; when he drew a circle on the face of the deep.

Who stretched a measuring line across it?
Surely you know!

marginNotes.author: Johann Heinrich Lambert
marginNotes.text:
The hyperbolic functions bear to the
equilateral hyperbola the same relation that the circular functions bear
to the circle.
